title: Turn a heading check into your first test
objective: Translate one manual expected result — a visible page heading — into one automated Playwright check: find the heading the way a visitor would, then assert that it is visible.
scenario: You're a manual QA engineer. One of your routine manual steps: open the community-garden signup page and confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is visible. Today you turn exactly that one step into an automated check. The page, a prepared test file with one empty test body, and a local test runner are ready. There is no AI in this exercise — the check is yours, written by hand.

chat.goalPrompt: Before we open anything — tell me in your own words: what are you here to get done today?
welcome: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — this is a practice space: everything is local and disposable, and nothing you do here can break anything real.

Here's the situation: you already check the community-garden signup page by hand — open it, confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is up there. Today that one manual step becomes your first automated check.

One honest warning from me: this exercise has no AI helper and no snippets to paste. The check will be yours, written by hand — that's the whole point. I'll point the way, but I won't write it for you.

tasks.text: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop and read README.md — it holds your manual check, written down, and the one idea this lesson teaches. You can also peek at the Garden Site window to see the page exactly as a visitor would.

Open tests/heading.spec.js in Code Studio. The navigation is already written; one empty test body is waiting. Turn your manual step into code: FIND the heading the way a visitor would notice it (by what it says, or that it's a heading), then STATE what you expect — that it should be visible. Two parts. Save with Ctrl+S.

Run `npm test` in the terminal. Read what it prints — it names your test and tells you what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation in it can come up green while proving nothing, so green alone isn't the goal.

When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, you're done — use "Check my work" and I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).

checkpoint.title: One manual step, now a real automated check — written by you

requirements.label: Only the prepared test slot was changed
Finds the heading the way a visitor would (role or text)
States the expectation: the heading is visible
The page itself was left exactly as seeded
Workspace is in a valid state

instructorNotes: VOICE: informal, calm, brief, plain words — a colleague at the next desk. Audience: a manual QA engineer with low technical confidence and NO AI familiarity, writing her first automated test. THE ONE LESSON: a check has two separate jobs — FIND the target the way a visitor perceives it (locator), then STATE the expectation (assertion). Never say 'selector', 'DOM', 'CSS', or introduce waits/fixtures/Git topics — none of them belong in this lesson. NEVER write or paste the completed check; the learner must author it (instructor-takeover is a scenario critical failure). Coach with questions first: 'your manual step has two halves — which half is in your code so far?'. Common beginner path (expected, not a failure): she writes the locator but no expectation — the run is GREEN anyway. If she believes she's done on green alone, ask what the test would say if the heading vanished. The checkpoint's verifier catches empty checks with teaching details; trust it, never claim completion yourself. Vocabulary to introduce gently and only after the plain-language version: locator ('how the test finds what a person would look at'), assertion ('the sentence that says what should be true'). There is deliberately no AI surface in this lab; if asked, say this one is hand-written on purpose — knowing the two-part shape is what makes AI-written tests reviewable later.
